title: Star Wars: The Roleplaying Game
content:
{{Top|rwm|leg|imp|reb|italics=1}}
{{Youmay|the D6 system roleplaying game from [[West End Games]]|the [[Star Wars Roleplaying Game|D20 system roleplaying game]] from [[Wizards of the Coast]]''' or '''the ''[[Star Wars Roleplaying]]'' series from [[Fantasy Flight Games]]}}
{{Cleanup}}
[[File:Star_Wars_RPG_2nd_Ed_Expanded.jpg|right|250px]]
'''''Star Wars: The Roleplaying Game''''', also known as '''''The Star Wars Roleplaying Game''''', was the first licensed [[roleplaying game]] set in the ''[[Star Wars]]'' universe. It was published by [[West End Games]] from [[1987]]<ref name="secrets">''[[The Secrets of Star Wars: Shadows of the Empire]]''</ref> until they lost the license in [[1998]]. A Swedish-language version was published under license by [[Äventyrsspel]], a French-language version was published under license by [[Jeux Descartes]], a [[Spanish]]-language version under license by [[Joc Internacional]], and a German-language version was published by [[Welt der Spiele]].

==Gameplay==
One player is the gamemaster who runs the gameworld, being something like the director and referee, while four to eight other players have a single character or role to play. Several events (like damage from fights) are determined by dice. Gameplays were short one-shot ''adventures'' or longer, more elaborate ''campaigns''.<ref name="secrets" /> The player characters have various skills and equipment and some can even use [[The Force/Legends|the Force]], albeit with the danger of turning to the [[Dark side of the Force|Dark Side]] if a character does not behave properly, at which the player would lose control of the character.

The rules of the game were developed by [[Greg Costikyan]].<ref name="secrets" /> Three versions of the main rulebook were published: [[Star Wars: The Roleplaying Game (first edition)|first edition]], [[Star Wars: The Roleplaying Game, Second Edition|second edition]], and [[Star Wars: The Roleplaying Game, Second Edition, Revised and Expanded|"Revised and Expanded" second edition]]. All three were based on West End Games' [[Wikipedia:D6 System|D6 System]].

The West End Games sourcebook collection is made of almost 100 books—not including second editions and compilations—plus 15 quarterly magazine issues under the title ''[[Star Wars Adventure Journal]]''.<ref name="secrets" /> They also edited several books related to, but not belonging to, the Roleplaying Game line, including [[Star Warriors: Starfighter Combat in the Star Wars Universe|''Star Warriors'']], the board games ''[[Escape from the Death Star]]'' and ''[[Assault on Hoth]]''; the two-player game [[Lightsaber duel/Legends|''Lightsaber Dueling'']], the ''[[Introductory Adventure Game]]'', ''[[Star Wars Miniatures Battles]]'', the ''[[Live-Action Adventures]]'' and single-player adventure books.

==Continuity==
The books contained not only game scenarios and guides, but also detailed descriptions (and expansions) of familiar locations, items, species, creatures and characters from the [[original trilogy]], along with new ones. The [[Clone Wars/Legends|Clone Wars]] was one of the themes that were off-limit to the publisher by [[Lucasfilm]].<ref name="secrets" /> As such, the lore produced for the RPG was one of the major sources of [[Star Wars Legends|Expanded Universe]] material before the publication of ''[[Heir to the Empire]]'' in 1991 sparked new interest in ''Star Wars'' publications, and remained an important source as well. Being one of the first sources giving numbers and data to the universe shown in the films, several of the statements given by the sourcebooks have been [[Retroactive continuity|retconned]] or corrected to better reflect the films and Expanded Universe fiction. Among the best known corrections are the continuity issues involving the [[Super Star Destroyer/Legends|Super Star Destroyers]] and the "discovery" of the [[Mon Calamari/Legends|Mon Calamari]] by the [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Galactic Empire]].

When Timothy Zahn was hired to write what became [[Star Wars: The Thrawn Trilogy|''The Thrawn Trilogy'']], he was sent a box of West End Games ''Star Wars'' books and directed to co-ordinate his writing with the background material already developed in the roleplaying game.<ref name="TTTS, Foreword">Timothy Zahn, in the Foreword to ''[[The Thrawn Trilogy Sourcebook]]''</ref> After the novel trilogy's success, West End Games created a [[The Thrawn Trilogy Sourcebook|series of sourcebooks]] inspired by Zahn's work.

==Legacy==
In [[1999]], West End Games lost the ''Star Wars'' RPG license. The new licensee, [[Wizards of the Coast]], began production of a [[Star Wars Roleplaying Game|new roleplaying game]] using their [[Wikipedia:d20 System|d20 System]]. As for West End Games, in [[2004]] they started publishing ''D6 Space'', a generic space opera game system based on the former ''Star Wars: The Roleplaying Game'', but not including specific ''Star Wars'' content. In 2012 [[Fantasy Flight Games]] released their own Star Wars [[Roleplaying game|RPG]] called [[Star Wars: Edge of the Empire|Edge of the Empire]].

In the early 1990s, before the promulgation of the modern Internet, the [[FidoNet Star Wars Echo]] ran a message forum for playing the West End Games ''Star Wars'' Roleplaying Game on-line on BBS.

In 2017, [[Fantasy Flight Games]] announced a [[Star Wars: The Roleplaying Game 30th Anniversary Edition|30th Anniversary Edition reprint]] of the first edition's [[Star Wars: The Roleplaying Game (first edition)|rulebook]] and [[The Star Wars Sourcebook|sourcebook]], scheduled originally for Q4 2017,<ref name="FFG">{{FantasyFlightGames|url=en/news/2017/8/16/dont-underestimate-the-force|text=Don't Underestimate the Force}}</ref> but ultimately delayed until its [[July 5]], [[2018]] release.<ref name="The Galaxy Awaits">{{FantasyFlightGames|url=en/news/2018/7/5/the-galaxy-awaits|text=The Galaxy Awaits}}</ref>
